data.title: EQT improves takeover offer for Intertek - FT
data.description:
EQT has increased its offer for Intertek (IKTSF) after an initial bid for the testing specialist was rejected this month.

EQT proposed a new bid of £54 a share earlier this week, according to a Financial Times report on Wednesday, which cited people familiar with the matter.  The offer is a ~5% increase to its original offer of £51.50 a share in cash.

QT’s latest bid values Intertek’s equity at more than £8.3bn, or ~£9.7bn including debt. 

Intertek said its board was reviewing EQT’s revised proposal with its advisers, according to the FT report.EQT declined to comment to the FT.

Intertek rose 2.3% in trading in London.

data.title: First Community GAAP EPS of $0.59, revenue of $5.49M
data.description:
• First Community press release (FCCO): Q1 GAAP EPS of $0.59.
  • Net income of $5.49M.
  • The company has also declared a cash dividend of $0.16 per common share.
  • Net interest margin on a tax equivalent basis of 3.27%.
  • Total loans increased during the first quarter of 2026 by $238.1 million to $1.549 billion.
  • Total deposits increased $298.7 million during the first quarter of 2026 to $2.048 billion.
  • Assets Under Management ((AUM)) were $1.130 billion.

data.title: AT&T expects $18B+ free cash flow in 2026 while targeting $4B annual cost savings by end of 2028
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: AT&T Inc. (T) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We executed well in the first quarter, delivering results that were consistent with the outlook we provided, while implementing several key strategic initiatives." (CEO, President & Chairman John Stankey)
  • "We reported 584,000 total fiber and fixed wireless advanced Internet customer net additions." (CEO, President & Chairman Stankey) "We closed our transaction with Lumen ahead of schedule, adding 1.1 million fiber customers and over 4 million fiber locations." (CEO, President & Chairman Stankey)
  • "After years of industry-leading investments in our fiber and wireless network, we believe that we have now established a structural advantage that others will not catch." (CEO, President & Chairman Stankey) "Today, we reach over 37 million customer locations with fiber, and we're on track to reach 60 million-plus locations by the end of the decade." (CEO, President & Chairman Stankey)
  • "Total revenues were up 2.9% year-over-year in the first quarter, and service revenues were up 1.4%." (Senior EVP & CFO Pascal Desroches) "Free cash flow was $2.5 billion, which is at the high end of the $2 billion to $2.5 billion outlook we provided in January." (Senior EVP & CFO Desroches)

Outlook

  • "We continue to expect we will grow consolidated service revenues in the low single-digit range for the full year." (Senior EVP & CFO Desroches) "We continue to expect consolidated adjusted EBITDA growth in the 3% to 4% range for the full year." (Senior EVP & CFO Desroches)
  • "Adjusted EPS of $0.57 in the first quarter was up nearly 12%, and we continue to expect full year adjusted EPS to be in the $2.25 to $2.35 range." (Senior EVP & CFO Desroches)
  • "For the second quarter, we expect free cash flow in the range of $4 billion to $4.5 billion, and we continue to expect $18 billion plus of free cash flows for the full year." (Senior EVP & CFO Desroches)

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted EBITDA was up 2.3% year-over-year in the first quarter, and adjusted EBITDA margin decreased 30 basis points to 37.4%." (Senior EVP & CFO Desroches) "As a reminder, our first quarter 2025 results included a benefit to adjusted EBITDA of approximately $100 million related to the resolution of vendor settlements." (Senior EVP & CFO Desroches)
  • "Free cash flow declined by roughly $600 million compared to last year, which was driven primarily by higher capital investment of $5.1 billion as we accelerate the pace of our fiber deployment." (Senior EVP & CFO Desroches)
  • "Wireless service revenues grew 1.7% year-over-year" and "294,000 postpaid phone net adds" were reported, while "Postpaid phone ARPU was flat versus a year ago." (Senior EVP & CFO Desroches)
  • "Advanced home internet net adds were 512,000" including "273,000 fiber net adds" and "239,000 Internet Air net adds." (Senior EVP & CFO Desroches) "Our outlook continues to anticipate an immaterial EBITDA contribution this year from the operating regions acquired from Lumen." (Senior EVP & CFO Desroches)

Q&A

  • John Hodulik, UBS: "First on OneConnect, can you talk about sort of how widely it will be rolled out?" and "Can that kind of improvement in phone churn that we've seen continue despite the increases from the Unlimited Your Way pricing?"; CEO Stankey: "this is going to be kind of an iteration rollout" and "I don't expect -- right now, sitting here today, I can tell you, massive amounts of volume on it in the first couple of weeks. We didn't expect that to be the case." (CEO, President & Chairman Stankey)
  • Michael Rollins, Citi: "Can you unpack how AT&T is defining the term open"; CEO Stankey: "the 2 most fundamental aspects of opening the network" were "opening aspects of our wireless network" and "the complete reengineering of the core of the network" to enable "a much broader set of APIs out into the public domain." (CEO, President & Chairman Stankey)
  • Sean Diffley, Morgan Stanley: "perceived threat from satellite" and "Would you ever consider doing MVNOs with emerging players?"; CEO Stankey: "12 to maybe 18 to 24 months from now, we will have always-on connectivity in the United States" and "my ideal outcome for the satellite space is that there's more than one satellite constellation up there." (CEO, President & Chairman Stankey)
  • David Barden, New Street: "the EchoStar Spectrum acquisition" and "update us on the copper retirement program"; CEO Stankey: "the improvement of performance in the network is noticeable" and "we have a path to shut down the infrastructure" with "30% of our wire centers" already on "a definitive schedule for shutdown." (CEO, President & Chairman Stankey)
  • Michael Ng, Goldman Sachs: "shifting away from device subsidies" and EBITDA acceleration drivers; CEO Stankey: "it's a balancing of the portfolio" and "I think we will gradually work our way through this over time." (CEO, President & Chairman Stankey) CFO Desroches: "we expect both service revenues and EBITDA to accelerate gradually" driven by "pricing actions that begins to take effect in April" and improving "performance of the Lumen asset." (Senior EVP & CFO Desroches)
  • Peter Supino, Wolfe: "AT&T reported 2.5 million DSL subs" and asked whether the fade affects fiber and pricing; CEO Stankey: "our fiber growth numbers have been relatively consistent" and "it's entirely economically rational and value creating" to go "from 40% to 50%, even if it means we take some ARPU dilution to do that." (CEO, President & Chairman Stankey)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative: analysts repeatedly pressed on competitive intensity and durability—"phone churn trend" (UBS), "perceived threat from satellite" (Morgan Stanley), and fiber economics as DSL fades (Wolfe).
  • Slightly positive: management emphasized execution and longer-term positioning—"We executed well in the first quarter" and "we're on track to reach 60 million-plus locations by the end of the decade." (CEO, President & Chairman Stankey)
  • Compared with last quarter, management maintained a similar confidence level on convergence and build plans, while the Q1 discussion added more implementation detail on Lumen integration and the OneConnect rollout cadence.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Segment reporting shift moved from planned to implemented: "beginning with our first quarter 2026 results" the company said "over 90% of our consolidated revenue and nearly all of our adjusted EBITDA is generated by our Advanced Connectivity segment." (Senior EVP & CFO Desroches)
  • The Lumen deal advanced from expected closing to completed: Q4 described agreements that "we continue to expect both these transactions to close early this year" (CEO & Chairman Stankey), while Q1 stated "We closed our transaction with Lumen ahead of schedule." (CEO, President & Chairman Stankey)
  • Q4 highlighted full-year momentum (e.g., "over 1.5 million postpaid phone net adds" in 2025), while Q1 emphasized near-term execution and integration, including that acquired Lumen regions had "an immaterial EBITDA contribution" in the quarter. (Senior EVP & CFO Desroches)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Free cash flow declined by roughly $600 million compared to last year" due to higher build spending, with mitigation framed as maintaining the annual outlook: "we continue to expect $18 billion plus of free cash flows for the full year." (Senior EVP & CFO Desroches)
  • Lumen profitability timing: "increased spending within these geographies" was cited as driving "an immaterial EBITDA contribution this year" from the acquired operating regions. (Senior EVP & CFO Desroches)
  • Legacy wind-down pressure: "Legacy service revenues declined about 25%" and "we expect this dynamic will persist for the next several quarters" due to migration/discontinuation timing. (Senior EVP & CFO Desroches)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as execution in line with prior expectations, led by record first-quarter advanced internet adds and higher fiber build investment, while reiterating full-year targets including $18B+ free cash flow and $2.25 to $2.35 adjusted EPS. Leadership emphasized that the Lumen fiber acquisition is already closed and being integrated, but is expected to be an immaterial EBITDA contributor this year as spending ramps, alongside continued focus on convergence offers like OneConnect, ongoing cost actions toward $4B annual savings by end of 2028, and a multi-year plan to expand fiber reach toward 60M+ locations by 2030.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Delta and United benefit from competitors cutting capacity amid higher fuel costs – analyst
data.description:
Delta Air Lines (DAL) and United Airlines (UAL) are emerging as the dominant “premium carriers” in the airline industry, benefiting significantly as lower-margin competitors are forced to cut capacity, according to Sheila Kahyaoglu, aerospace and defense analyst at Jefferies.

“Good for Delta (DAL) and United (UAL), bad for the rest of the industry at the moment,” Kahyaoglu said, noting that carriers with weaker margins are being eliminated from the competitive landscape in both the U.S. and European markets.

In an interview with CNBC, Kahyaoglu detailed the pricing power these two airlines are commanding. Pricing for both Delta (DAL) and United (UAL) was roughly flat in the fourth quarter but is expected to rise 6%-7% in the first quarter and reach double digits by the second quarter.

The analyst noted that these carriers are successfully passing along higher jet fuel costs to consumers, though not as fully as they would like until later in the year.

On the topic of potential industry consolidation, Kahyaoglu suggested that Delta (DAL) and United (UAL) are unlikely to pursue mergers anytime soon. Both carriers are currently at approximately two times leverage and are focused on bringing that figure down.

“I don’t see either Delta or United pulling any triggers anytime soon,” she said, adding that they are more likely to benefit from struggling competitors cutting capacity or consolidating.

Kahyaoglu also provided an update on Boeing’s (BA) operational performance, noting that production is finally on track. The 737 is now at 42 aircraft per month, headed to 47 by summer, while the 787 has stabilized at eight per month—production rates not seen since 2019. Free cash flow came in approximately $1B better than expected, driven by advances on the defense side and net orders.

Looking ahead, the analyst outlined a bull case for Boeing (BA) in the 2028-2029 timeframe centered on aircraft pricing power.

Using Southwest (LUV) as an example, she estimated that 737 prices could rise from $25M per aircraft today to $42M by 2028.

“As Boeing (BA) delivers on time without late slots, you could see how the bull case on June goes from $6B of cash to $12B on just one aircraft platform,” she explained.

Overall, Kahyaoglu expressed a sense of growing predictability at Boeing under current leadership. She noted that the Middle East represents only 14% of Boeing’s (BA) backlog, with two-thirds of those orders slated for post-2030, meaning minimal near-term impact from that region. Carriers are now actively seeking earlier slots to secure more fuel-efficient aircraft, reflecting renewed confidence in Boeing’s (BA) delivery schedule.

data.title: GE Vernova forecasts $6.5B-$7.5B 2026 free cash flow as backlog target moves to 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: GE Vernova (GEV) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We've had a solid start to '26" and "in the last 90 days, we've added $13 billion to our total backlog and now expect to reach $200 billion in backlog in '27 versus our previous expectation of '28" (CEO Scott Strazik).
  • "In Gas Power, we continue to see significant demand and favorable pricing trends for both equipment and services" and "we saw continued strength in new gas turbine agreements in Q1, signing 21 gigawatts"; Strazik added, "we now expect to book 10 to 15 gigawatts of contracts in Q2 and to end '26 with at least 110 gigawatts under contract" (CEO Strazik).
  • "In Electrification... data centers... accounted for approximately $2.4 billion in orders in Q1, more than the full year of '25" and "our HVDC backlog represents approximately $10 billion" (CEO Strazik).
  • "We delivered a strong start to 2026 with robust orders, growing backlog and revenues, margin expansion, and significant free cash flow generation" (Chief Financial Officer Kenneth Parks).

Outlook

  • "For the full year, we're raising our guidance" to revenue of "$44.5 billion to $45.5 billion" and "adjusted EBITDA margin... to 12% to 14%" (CFO Kenneth Parks).
  • "We're increasing our 2026 free cash flow guidance to between $6.5 billion and $7.5 billion, up from $5 billion to $5.5 billion" (CFO Parks).
  • Segment updates embedded in the full-year guide included: "we now anticipate Power EBITDA margins to be between 17% to 19%, up from our previous range of 16% to 18%" and "we're raising our revenue expectations... in Electrification... to $14 billion to $14.5 billion" with Electrification EBITDA margin "to 18% to 20%, up from 17% to 19%" (CFO Parks).
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s call, the company increased full-year 2026 targets that were previously stated as "$44 billion to $45 billion" of revenue, "11% to 13%" adjusted EBITDA margin, and "$5 billion" to "$5.5 billion" of free cash flow (CFO Parks, Q4 2025 call).

Financial Results

  • "In the first quarter, we booked orders of $18.3 billion" and "revenue increased 7%" (CFO Parks).
  • "Adjusted EBITDA grew 87% year-over-year to $896 million" and "the strong adjusted EBITDA and working capital management drove $4.8 billion of free cash flow in the first quarter" (CFO Parks).
  • Portfolio and capital allocation items highlighted on the call included: "we completed the acquisition of the remaining 50% ownership stake of Prolec for $5.3 billion" and "we completed the sale of our manufacturing software business for approximately $600 million of pretax proceeds" (CFO Parks).

Q&A

  • Mark W. Strouse, JPMorgan Chase & Co: "your latest thoughts on Gas Power capacity" and "lead times"; CEO Strazik: "we're directionally at about 3 years lead time today" and "we still have about 10 gigawatts remaining cumulatively in '29 and '30 together".
  • Julian Mitchell, Barclays: "help us understand... why you think you're so well placed" in Power Transmission and "any issues or major tariff mitigation" for Prolec; CEO Strazik: "we're attaching that electrical equipment to the power generation solutions"; CFO Parks: "We guided to $250 million to $350 million net impact on the company in 2026" and "with how the 232s have been defined, there's a little bit more impact on the Prolec numbers".
  • Nicole DeBlase, Deutsche Bank: "update on the pricing environment" beyond the "10 to 20" point data point; CEO Strazik: "we continue to be in that 10- to 20-point growth in price on new bidding and winning activity" and said the "100 gigawatts we have on contract today is with almost 90 distinct customers in 24 different countries".
  • Andrew Kaplowitz, Citi: progress on data center Electrification "entitlement"; CEO Strazik: "we've already secured a second order... in April" for EMS and "we'll deliver the first product to a hyperscaler in the fall of this year" for solid-state transformers.
  • David Arcaro, Morgan Stanley: "customer appetite for framework agreements"; CEO Strazik: "We have not closed one of those transactions to date" and referenced "30 to 35 framework agreements" under discussion.
  • Joseph Ritchie, Goldman Sachs: whether the company will "limit" orders beyond 2030; CEO Strazik: "we do continue to expect to take on orders for 31 and beyond"; CFO Parks: "we'll continue to add capacity... by continuing to grow our lean initiatives".
  • Alexander Virgo, Evercore ISI: Vietnam order concerns and April orders clarification; CEO Strazik: "our 4.8 gigawatts... are all continuing to progress"; CFO Parks: "in April, we have booked power equipment orders a value equivalent to what we booked in the full first quarter".

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was slightly skeptical on sustainability and constraints, pressing on capacity/lead times, tariff exposure, framework agreements, and Vietnam project risk, including "not taking orders beyond 2030" and whether to "limit any type of order intake" (Joseph Ritchie, Goldman Sachs).
  • Management sentiment was positive in prepared remarks and largely confident in Q&A, using statements such as "we do continue to expect to take on orders for 31 and beyond" and "we feel better and better... about our ability to meet this moment for the long term" (CEO Scott Strazik).
  • Versus last quarter, the tone remained confident on gas pricing and contracts ("another 10 to 20 points of pricing strength in the SRAs" was emphasized in Q4, while Q1 reiterated "10- to 20-point growth in price" on new activity), and the biggest directional change was Q1’s raised full-year guide and accelerated backlog timeline (CEO Strazik; CFO Parks).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 introduced reporting realignments (Power steam integrated "primarily into our nuclear business" and Electrification split into "4 distinct business units"), while Q4 focused on incorporating Prolec into 2026 guidance and the 2028 outlook (VP IR Michael Lapides).
  • Q1 moved the backlog milestone forward as Strazik said, "now expect to reach $200 billion in backlog in '27 versus our previous expectation of '28"; Q4 emphasized ending 2025 at "$150 billion" backlog and expectations to reach "approximately 100 gigawatts under contract in '26" (CEO Strazik, Q4 2025 call).
  • The biggest guidance shift from Q4 to Q1 was the higher full-year 2026 free cash flow range and higher margin ranges for Power and Electrification (CFO Parks in both calls).
  • Analyst focus shifted from Q4’s offshore wind stop-work/Vineyard Wind overhang to Q1’s gas capacity, Electrification data center momentum, tariff math (including Section 232), and how far out gas contracting extends (analyst Q&A in both calls).

Risks and Concerns

  • "the tariff landscape has continued to move" and the company maintained a net tariff outlook of "$250 million to $350 million" for 2026, while noting "a little bit more impact on the Prolec numbers" (CFO Kenneth Parks).
  • On geopolitical disruption risk, Strazik said the company has seen "minimal impact to our business and financial performance to date" from "recent conflicts in the Middle East" (CEO Scott Strazik).
  • On Wind, Parks stated "Wind EBITDA losses were $382 million in the quarter, in line with our expectations" and cited "the impact of tariffs at Onshore Wind" and "higher contract losses at Offshore Wind" as drivers (CFO Parks).

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as a stronger-than-expected start supported by accelerating orders and backlog expansion, and raised full-year 2026 guidance, particularly for free cash flow, while emphasizing continued global Gas Power contracting, fast-growing Electrification demand tied to data centers and HVDC, and ongoing exposure to tariffs and Wind losses that the company said are incorporated into its outlook.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: India locks in costlier urea supplies as Middle East conflict jolts fertilizer market
data.description:
India, the largest global buyer of urea, has agreed to purchase the fertilizer at significantly higher prices than in its last tender, reflecting supply disruptions tied to conflict in the Middle East and a sharp rise in global benchmark prices.

Indian Potash Ltd., which handles imports on behalf of the government, is set to buy 1.5 million metric tons for delivery to India’s west coast at $935 per ton, along with another 1 million tons for the east coast at $959 per ton, Bloomberg News reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

Those prices are nearly double the levels India secured before the regional conflict escalated, the people said, requesting anonymity because the talks are commercially sensitive. Prior to the outbreak of hostilities, Middle East-origin urea, a key source for India, was trading near $490 per ton, according to Green Markets data.

More than 24 companies submitted bids in the recent tender, with offers ranging from $935 to $1,136 per ton. The purchase would mark India’s first major fertilizer procurement since U.S.-Israel strikes on Iran and arrives at a critical moment before planting season for monsoon crops including rice, corn and soybeans.

Although the 2.5 million-ton tender has not yet been officially finalized, market participants expect confirmation by Thursday.

India’s domestic urea production depends heavily on natural gas, much of it sourced from the Middle East and used to produce ammonia, a core ingredient in fertilizer manufacturing. Supply strains intensified after effective disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz prompted some producers in the region to temporarily shut plants last month.

Indian officials are now negotiating directly with major suppliers and exporters to secure additional shipments of both nitrogen-based and phosphate fertilizers.

Urea prices worldwide have climbed sharply since the conflict began. Bloomberg Intelligence estimates that roughly 45% of global supply moves through the Persian Gulf, leaving the market vulnerable to any prolonged shipping or production interruptions.

data.title: Copper rises after Trump extends Iran ceasefire; aluminum seen facing 'black swan' supply shock
data.description:
Copper prices rose Wednesday as President Trump's extension of the ceasefire with Iran ​improved overall risk sentiment, although lingering uncertainty over the war capped gains.

The renewed premium of Comex copper ​prices over the London Metal Exchange global benchmark, a defining feature of ​the copper market in 2025, has re-emerged this month, encouraging ⁠shipments to the U.S.

More copper will flow to the U.S. ​while the price premium persists until July, when a decision is expected ​on whether the U.S. will impose tariffs on the metal, Mercuria's global head of metals Kostas Bintas told Reuters.

Benchmark three-month copper (HG1:COM) on the London Metal Exchange recently traded at ~$13,270/metric ton after touching a record peak of $14,527/ton on January 29, while LME aluminum (LMAHDS03:COM) rose 1.3% to $3,604/ton after reaching a four-year high at $3,672/ton on April 16.

Goldman Sachs reiterated its copper price forecast to average $12,650/metric ton this year and its estimate of a 490K-ton 2026 surplus for the metal.

However, the bank said the disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, combined with China's decision to ban sulfuric acid exports starting May 1, could tighten a market critical for copper production.

Additionally, China's ban on sulfuric acid exports lasting through year-end would put 200K tons of Chilean production at risk, equivalent to 1% of global supply, as the country sourced roughly a third of its acid from China in 2025, the bank added.

Meanwhile, ​the global aluminum ​market is already ⁠experiencing a "black swan" supply shock due to disruptions from the war, which likely will cause a supply shock that will lead to major shortages this year, according to the top metals analyst at commodity trader Mercuria.

"The scale of the supply shock ​we're seeing in the aluminum market is probably the largest single supply shock a base ⁠metals market has suffered in the post-2000 era," Mercuria's Nick Snowdon told Reuters at the Financial Times conference.

Mercuria estimates the market ​will face a minimum deficit of ~2M tons between now and year-end, which Snowdon said may prove conservative, as it assumes a near-term improvement in alumina flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

Freeport-McMoRan (FCX) up 3.4% in Wednesday's trading to an all-time intraday high of $70.49; other potentially relevant stocks include Southern Copper (SCCO), Ero Copper (ERO), Taseko Mines (TGB), Teck Resources (TECK), Hudbay Minerals (HBM), Alcoa (AA), Century Aluminum (CENX), Kaiser Aluminum (KALU), Constellium (CSTM), BHP (BHP), Rio Tinto (RIO), Vale (VALE).

data.title: Stock rally getting harder to chase - Goldman
data.description:
Goldman Sachs strategist Tony Pasquariello said Wednesday the stock market’s (SPY) primary uptrend remains intact but warned that near-term conditions have grown more challenging.

“The [relative strength index] of S&P is 70, and the most intense spec buying is behind us now, so the short-term setup is certainly more demanding than it was at the end of March,” Pasquariello wrote in a note to clients. An RSI reading above 70 indicates overbought.

The strategist laid out a detailed bear case, arguing that “stock operators are complacent” and that any “impingement on the flow of energy is set to cascade” as the Iran conflict continues. He also pointed to “a sharp deceleration in CTA demand” and noted the rally has been “exceptionally narrow,” with “only 5 percent of the stocks in the Russell 3000 index (IWV)” trading at 52-week highs. Additionally, he warned that “US GDP growth is set to slow considerably in the second half of the year as tax stimuli fades.”

However, Pasquariello gave equal weight to bullish factors, noting that “the trading community still has room to add incremental risk” and that “the largest owner of the asset class, US households, never lost the trail and continue to sponsor stocks.”

His bottom line struck a cautious tone without turning outright bearish: “The primary bull trend is intact, but tactical risk/reward is not overly compelling.”

Pasquariello recommended maintaining a “long delta/long vol” positioning and cautioned that with markets “leaning heavily” on expectations for a Middle East resolution, “deeper downside tails look underpriced again.” He suggested “maintaining, and potentially increasing, hedges makes sense.”

data.title: Wabtec raises 2026 adjusted EPS outlook to $10.25-$10.65 while holding revenue guidance unchanged
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Westinghouse Air Brake Technologies (WAB) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “The team delivered a strong first quarter with operational results ahead of our expectations,” and “sales were $3 billion, which was up 13% and adjusted EPS was up 19% from the year ago quarter,” with “total cash flow from operations for the quarter…$199 million,” according to (CEO Rafael Santana).
  • “Backlog remains a key strength,” including “12-month backlog…up 13% from the prior year,” and “the multiyear backlog exceeded $30 billion, up 38%,” (CEO Santana) said, adding that the company “secured a multibillion-dollar multiyear mining order,” a “$210 million multiyear modernization with MBTA,” and “a $54 million brake and couplers order with Kawasaki for the New York City Transit.”
  • “This performance included the impact of an exit from a low-margin Digital project, which was fully reflected in the quarter,” and “sales for the first quarter were $2.95 billion,” while “adjusted operating margin for Q1 was 21.9%,” (CFO John Olin) said.

Outlook

  • “We are increasing our previous adjusted EPS midpoint guidance, and we now expect adjusted EPS to be in the range of $10.25 to $10.65,” while “our revenue guidance remains unchanged,” (CEO Rafael Santana) said.
  • “Any tariffs that have been announced up to this point are included in the guidance,” and “we’re not seeing any impact with regards to revenues,” (CEO Santana) said; (CFO John Olin) added that for Section 232, “there is no difference. We’re largely indifferent between that from a financial standpoint.”

Financial Results

  • (CFO John Olin) said “GAAP earnings per diluted share was $2.12,” and “adjusted earnings per diluted share was $2.71,” while “we had net pretax charges of $41 million for purchase accounting adjustments and transaction costs…as well as restructuring costs.”
  • (CFO Olin) said “equipment sales were up 52.5%,” “services sales were down 17.3%,” “component sales were down 6.3%,” and “Digital Intelligence sales were up 75.7%,” while adding for Transit that “sales were up 17.8%.”
  • (CFO Olin) reported (i) Freight: “adjusted operating margin in the Freight segment was 26.0%,” and Freight “12-month backlog was $6.68 billion” with “multiyear backlog of $25.18 billion”; and (ii) Transit: “sales were up 17.8% at $835 million,” with “adjusted operating income as a percent of revenue…16.6%,” and “Transit 12-month backlog…$2.57 billion.”
  • (CFO Olin) said liquidity ended at “$2.09 billion,” net debt leverage was “2.3x,” and “we repurchased $242 million of our shares and paid $53 million in dividends.”

Q&A

  • Ken Hoexter, BofA: Tariff mitigation and order impact? (CEO Rafael Santana): “Any tariffs that have been announced up to this point are included in the guidance,” and “we’re not seeing any impact with regards to revenues.” (CFO John Olin): On Section 232, “there is no difference,” and “the new tariff regime is certainly much easier to administer.”
  • Ken Hoexter, BofA: What drove the $0.20 EPS midpoint increase? (CFO John Olin): “Roughly half of it…$0.10 is due to the operational side…[and] the other $0.10 is due to the nonoperational,” adding “other income…$23 million is largely going to stick,” while tax timing in Q1 was favorable but “we still expect the 24.5% full year rate.”
  • Angel Castillo Malpica, Morgan Stanley: Why keep revenue guidance unchanged despite backlog? (CEO Rafael Santana): Headwinds include “Freight car deliveries potentially being further down,” “inflation in our input cost,” and “electronics,” while positives include “acquisitions being ahead of plan,” “new product introductions,” and “incremental demand on existing projects.”
  • Scott Group, Wolfe: How much backlog growth is acquisition-driven and what about Q2 cadence? (CFO John Olin): Dellner contributed “about 3 percentage points” to enterprise 12-month backlog growth; on Q2, “the second quarter is going to look a lot like the…first quarter…with the exception of the nonoperational items we don’t expect to repeat.”
  • Benjamin Mohr Mok, Citigroup: Backlog conversion timing and organic cadence; Alstom risk? (CFO John Olin): 12-month backlog is “not a straight and direct line,” but “over time, it is going to emulate what our revenue growth is”; (CEO Santana) on Alstom: “I’m not going to comment on any customer specifics,” and “project delays…have been a reality.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone slightly negative, concentrated on tariffs, backlog conversion, and guidance mechanics, including Ken Hoexter’s question on whether Q1 “huge tax benefit” and “below-the-line gain” explain the guidance change.
  • Management tone slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, emphasizing execution and mitigation, including (CEO Rafael Santana) saying “we’re not seeing any impact with regards to revenues,” and (CFO John Olin) describing a split of “$0.10” operational and “$0.10” nonoperational in the EPS raise.
  • Versus Q4, the discussion shifted from initiating 2026 guidance to updating it; the Q1 call carried more tactical Q&A focus on tariffs and the EPS uplift drivers, while Q4 carried more emphasis on shareholder returns actions and multi-year program framing.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 raised 2026 adjusted EPS guidance to “$10.25 to $10.65” while Q4 guided “$10.05 to $10.45,” and Q1 reiterated that “our revenue guidance remains unchanged” from the prior outlook.
  • Q1 management repeatedly framed tariffs as included and operationally mitigated (“business as usual”), while Q4 highlighted tariffs as a rising headwind with impacts “growing exponentially” into the first half of 2026.
  • Analyst focus in Q1 concentrated on tariffs mechanics, the nonoperational contribution to the guidance raise, and backlog conversion cadence; Q4 analyst questions leaned more toward cash conversion guidance changes, modernization/new-build mix, and the acquisition environment.

Risks and Concerns

  • (CFO John Olin) said the company expects “margin pressure in the first half of the year because of tariffs,” while (CEO Rafael Santana) cited “inflation in our input cost,” “electronics,” and “obsolescence” as headwinds.
  • (CFO Olin) also said “40% are not covered by price escalators,” with input pressures including “copper, aluminum, steel,” “precious metals,” “silver,” and “transportation costs,” plus “pressure on memory chips in our Digital business.”

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as operationally ahead of plan, with year-over-year sales and adjusted EPS growth and backlog expansion supporting visibility. The company held revenue guidance steady but raised the 2026 adjusted EPS range to $10.25-$10.65, with management attributing the midpoint increase to both operational improvement and nonoperational items, while reiterating that announced tariffs are incorporated into guidance and that it is not seeing revenue impact from tariffs to date.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Weatherford projects 2026 revenue $4.5B-$4.95B and adjusted EBITDA $945M-$1.075B amid Iran conflict disruptions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Weatherford International plc (WFRD) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "To summarize our Q1 2026 performance, we delivered revenue of $1.152 billion, adjusted EBITDA of $233 million at a 20.2% margin and adjusted free cash flow of $85 million." (President, CEO & Director Girish Saligram)
  • "I would also like to highlight our announcement during the quarter of a proposal to re-domesticate from Ireland to the United States, specifically Texas, which we believe will simplify our corporate structure, enhance capital management flexibility and support long-term shareholder value creation." (President, CEO & Director Saligram)
  • "Since the start of the recent Iran conflict...our priority has been the safety and security of our employees and ensuring business continuity to the extent it was feasible." (President, CEO & Director Saligram)
  • "We are also taking further actions to fine-tune our portfolio through a series of small noncore divestitures." (President, CEO & Director Saligram)
  • "In the first quarter, we generated $85 million of adjusted free cash flow, representing a 36.5% adjusted free cash flow conversion." (Executive VP & CFO Anuj Dhruv)

Outlook

  • "Turning to second quarter 2026 guidance...we expect revenues to be in the range of $1.017 billion to $1.110 billion and adjusted EBITDA to be between $195 million and $220 million." (Executive VP & CFO Dhruv)
  • "For the full year 2026...Revenues are now expected to be in the range of $4.5 billion to $4.95 billion, and adjusted EBITDA is expected to be in the range of $945 million to $1.075 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Dhruv)
  • "Adjusted free cash flow conversion is now expected to be in the mid-40% range" and "our effective tax rate is expected to be in the low to mid-20% range for 2026." (Executive VP & CFO Dhruv)
  • "We believe the conflict would result in about $30 million to $50 million profit impact over the first half of the year." (President, CEO & Director Saligram)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s full-year ranges, management said 2026 guidance was "refining...to reflect the impact of the Iran conflict in the first half." (Executive VP & CFO Dhruv)

Financial Results

  • "Revenue declined 3% on a year-on-year basis...predominantly driven by the divestiture of the Pressure Pumping business in Argentina." (President, CEO & Director Saligram)
  • "On a sequential basis, revenues were down 11%, reflecting typical first quarter seasonality and the conflict in Iran." (President, CEO & Director Saligram)
  • "Importantly, our Q1 working capital efficiency improved by approximately 100 basis points sequentially" and "our adjusted net working capital as a percentage of revenues was 27.9% in the first quarter." (President, CEO & Director Saligram; Executive VP & CFO Dhruv)
  • "During the first quarter, CapEx was $54 million or 4.7% of revenues" and "at the end of the first quarter, we had approximately $1.05 billion of cash and restricted cash, and our net leverage ratio remained well below 0.5x." (Executive VP & CFO Dhruv)
  • "In the first quarter of 2026, we returned $30 million to shareholders comprising $20 million in dividends and $10 million in share repurchases." (Executive VP & CFO Dhruv)

Q&A

  • John Anderson, Barclays: asked about the "pretty big shift in tone" and "structural shift"; President, CEO & Director Saligram: "we feel that the mid- to long term is incredibly positive for the sector" and "this will lead to structurally higher oil prices and LNG prices."
  • Scott Gruber, Citigroup: asked which Middle East countries and product lines were most impacted; President, CEO & Director Saligram: "In Oman, for the most part, operations have been fairly normal" while "In Iraq, there has been some suspension of projects" and in "Saudi Arabia and the UAE...the biggest impact" was "on the offshore side."
  • James West, Melius: asked about countries restarting operations; President, CEO & Director Saligram: "we are starting to see a little bit of a normalization" but "until the strait fully opens...it's going to be very difficult to get back to that full sense of normalcy."
  • Saurabh Pant, BofA: asked about Mexico activity and payment mechanism; Executive VP & CFO Dhruv: "They put in a $13 billion mechanism for payments" and "the total balance...is about $283 million as of March 31."
  • Derek Podhaizer, Piper Sandler: asked to quantify lost revenue vs. elevated costs; President, CEO & Director Saligram: "it’s really sort of order of magnitude, 60-40 from a revenue cost standpoint."
  • James Rollyson, Raymond James: asked about re-domestication benefits; Executive VP & CFO Dhruv: "It increases shareholder value" and "will not impact day-to-day operations."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts slightly positive overall, with focus on structural opportunity and near-term disruption, including "The world has changed." (John Anderson, Barclays)
  • Management positive in prepared remarks but acknowledged near-term uncertainty, saying "the immediate couple of months are a bit murky" while also stating "we believe the industry is entering a period of multiyear visibility." (President, CEO & Director Saligram)
  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s tone shifted from a "tale of 2 halves" framing to describing Q1 as "the most severe disruption to the physical oil market in the industry's history." (President, CEO & Director Saligram)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance ranges were revised from Q4’s full-year revenue range of "$4.6 billion to $5.05 billion" and adjusted EBITDA of "$980 million to $1.12 billion" to Q1’s "$4.5 billion to $4.95 billion" and "$945 million to $1.075 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Dhruv)
  • The key strategic addition in Q1 was the corporate move: "a proposal to re-domesticate from Ireland to the United States"; the prior quarter emphasized transformation initiatives and country opportunities such as Venezuela. (President, CEO & Director Saligram)
  • Analyst emphasis shifted from Saudi recovery and Venezuela optionality to direct interrogation of Middle East operational disruption, cost/revenue split, and timing of normalization. (Multiple analysts)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Freight costs have risen dramatically" and "there are both delays and higher costs in moving materials and people." (President, CEO & Director Saligram)
  • "Even right now...the notion of the strait being completely open to passage is not being manifested in reality." (President, CEO & Director Saligram)
  • Management’s mitigation included maintaining readiness: "we are not having a knee-jerk reaction on personnel" and emphasized regional capability: "we have local capabilities in each country...our flagship manufacturing." (President, CEO & Director Saligram)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a quarter with revenue of $1.152 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $233 million, while attributing the step-down in the Q2 outlook and the refinement of full-year ranges to disruptions tied to the Iran conflict and related logistics and activity impacts. Executives emphasized continued collections in Mexico (including a $13 billion payment mechanism and a $283 million balance as of March 31), a proposed re-domestication to Texas, ongoing portfolio pruning, and a view that the second half of 2026 and 2027 could improve if Middle East operations normalize.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
